steps.m: to shine brightly in the morning

steps.n: Added vowel marks ُ and َ to show it's a noun. The و at the end becomes ى because it's a weak letter at the end of a word.
Added ال to make it specific. The ض gets a shadda (ّ) because it comes after ال
Added وَ (and) at the start. The اَل changes to ٱل when it comes after و. Added ٰ to show the ى is pronounced long.
Connected the root letters together with fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to form the basic word for night
Adding ال makes it specific (the night), and the ِ at the end shows it connects with what comes after it in the ayah (إِذَا سَجَىٰ - when it covers)
Adding و at the beginning means 'and'
This is the basic form of the word that means a specific point in time

steps.m: when (connects to سَجَىٰ - it grows still)
steps.n: Adding ا at the end makes it more general and conditional, perfect for describing 'when' something happens. Here it introduces what happens with the night - when it grows still.

steps.m: to be dark, quiet, or still (like night)

steps.n: Added fathas to make past tense. The و changes to ا because it's at the end after a fatha.
Changed spelling of alif to ya, but keeps same meaning
This is a negative particle that makes the verb وَدَّعَكَ (has forsaken you) into 'has not forsaken you'. It's one of the basic building blocks of Arabic that doesn't come from a root.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Adding shadda makes the meaning more intense or emphatic
Added كَ at the end to show who is being bid farewell to

steps.m: to nurture, raise, sustain, or be lord over

steps.n: Root letters combined with fatha and doubled middle letter to make 'lord/master'
Added ُ (damma) before كَ to show this is the subject doing the action. This word is the subject of وَدَّعَكَ (has forsaken you) in this ayah.
Added fatha to show proper pronunciation
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before. This مَا is used to negate the verb قَلَىٰ (has forsaken) in this ayah.
Added fathas (َ) after each letter and changed the last letter to ى to show this happened in the past
Added the small alif (ٰ) above the ى to show this is how the word ends. This word is connected to وَدَّعَكَ (he bid farewell to you) in this ayah - both actions are being denied by مَا (not).

steps.m: to come after, to be later

steps.n: Adding آ at start makes it mean 'more/most late' or 'last'
Adding ة makes it feminine and creates the specific meaning of 'the hereafter'
Added emphatic لَ to add certainty to the statement
Added damma to show this is the subject of the sentence
The ل at the start adds emphasis, like saying 'truly' or 'indeed'

steps.m: and surely the hereafter (subject of خَيْرٌ)
steps.n: Added conjunction و to connect with previous statement. This word is the subject of خَيْرٌ (better) in this ayah.
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and make it a noun. The middle letter gets a sukun (ْ) because it's a weak letter ي.
Added double dammah to show this is an indefinite noun in the nominative case
Connected the basic particles together with vowels to make them readable

steps.m: surely for you (connected to خَيْرٌ - better)
steps.n: Added emphasis with shadda. This word connects to خَيْرٌ (better) in the ayah, showing that the afterlife is surely better for the Prophet ﷺ.
This is the basic form of the preposition that shows comparison or origin. In this ayah, it's used to compare the hereafter (الآخرة) with what comes after this word.
Added fatha at the end to connect this word with الأولى (the first/worldly life) that comes after it. This connection shows we're comparing the hereafter with the worldly life.

steps.m: to come first/be before something else

steps.n: The ى at the end makes this word feminine, like how 'actress' is the feminine form of 'actor' in English

steps.m: the first (life/world) (contrasted with وَلَلْءَاخِرَةُ - the last/next life)
steps.n: Adding ٱلْ makes it specific. In this ayah, it refers to the present worldly life in contrast to the afterlife mentioned before it.

steps.m: the first (life/world) (contrasted with وَلَلْءَاخِرَةُ - the last/next life)
steps.n: The small alif (ٰ) is added as a pronunciation guide to show the long 'aa' sound at the end
This is a particle that indicates future tense, similar to 'will' in English

steps.m: surely will (in the future of يُعْطِيكَ)
steps.n: Adding لَ makes it stronger, showing that this will definitely happen. This emphasizes that Allah will surely give (يُعْطِيكَ) in the future.

steps.m: and surely will (in the future of يُعْطِيكَ)
steps.n: وَ connects this phrase to what came before it, showing this is part of a sequence of promises
Connected the root letters with fatha marks. The و changes to ا at the end because it's a weak letter.
Added يُ at start to show 'he' is doing it now. Changed vowels to show ongoing action. The ا changes to ي at the end.

steps.m: he gives you (object of رَبُّكَ's giving)
steps.n: Added كَ at the end to show you are receiving the giving. This word is connected to رَبُّكَ (your Lord) who is doing the giving.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form a basic past tense verb
The root verb 'to nurture' becomes 'Lord' by doubling the middle letter, showing someone who nurtures and cares for everything
Added damma to show this is the doer of the action in the sentence
Added كَ to show this is your Lord. This Lord is the one who will give (يُعْطِيكَ) in this verse.
The root letters were connected with vowels to make a past tense verb meaning 'he was pleased'. The و at the end became ي which is common in Arabic verbs.
Added ت at start to mean 'you'. Changed to future tense by adding ى at the end. Shows 'you will' instead of 'he was'.

steps.m: and then you will be pleased (result of يُعْطِيكَ رَبُّكَ - your Lord giving to you)
steps.n: Added ف to show this happens as a result of what came before. The ٰٓ marks the end of the word and extends the sound.
Added مْ to make it negative for past tense verbs
Added أَ at the beginning to make it a question. This type of question expects the answer 'yes'
Basic past tense form showing someone did the action
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense form
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels. The و drops because it's a weak letter at the start.
Added sukun to show jussive mood because this verb comes after لَمْ (did not) in أَلَمْ

steps.m: find you (as the object of أَلَمْ)
steps.n: Added كَ to show that 'you' are what is being found. This connects to أَلَمْ (did He not) at the start of the ayah.
The root letters ي-ت-م form an adjective meaning orphaned
Changed from verb 'to be fatherless' into noun 'orphan' by adding vowels to make يَتِيم, which describes someone who lost their father

Added ًا at the end to show this is what is being found in the phrase 'Did He not find you an orphan?' The ًا shows both that it's not specific (any orphan) and that it's being acted upon by the verb find.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Added فَ at the beginning to show this happened after something else mentioned before
Added long alif after first letter to show emphasis/causation
Connected the root letters with fathas to make a past tense verb
Added ك at the end to show who was found (you)

steps.m: and he found you (followed by ضَآلًّا meaning 'lost')
steps.n: Added و with fatha at the beginning to mean 'and'. This connects this word to what came before it. The word ضَآلًّا (lost) comes after to describe your state when found.
Added basic vowels to connect the letters and show past tense

steps.m: one who is lost (found by وَجَدَ)
steps.n: Changed from 'he wandered off' (verb) to 'lost person' (describes someone). Found by وَجَدَ in this ayah.
The آ (alif mamduda) emphasizes the state of being lost
The tanween fatha makes the word indefinite and marks it as the object of the verb
Added fathas and changed ي to ى because it's at the end of the word. This shows the action happened in the past.

steps.m: and so he showed the way (after finding you lost in 93:7:2)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this happened as a result of Allah finding the Prophet lost (mentioned in the previous part of the verse)

steps.m: and so He guided (you) (after finding you lost in 93:7:2)
steps.n: Added the small alif (ٰ) at the end to show proper pronunciation. This is just a pronunciation helper and doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: to find or come across something

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fathas to show Allah's action in the past
Adding conjunction prefix و meaning 'and'

steps.m: and He (Allah) found you (Prophet Muhammad ﷺ)
steps.n: Added ك to show that Prophet Muhammad ﷺ is who was found. This connects to the next verse عَآئِلًا (in poverty) which describes his condition.

steps.m: to lack resources or be in need

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Changed to active participle form showing who does the action. The weak letter ي becomes ا
Makes the word stronger/longer by adding a long alif
Added tanween alif to show this word is the object being acted upon in the phrase 'He found you poor'
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb
Added أَ at beginning and sukun on middle letter to show making someone else wealthy

steps.m: and so He made (you) wealthy (after وَوَجَدَكَ عَآئِلًا)
steps.n: Added فَ to show this follows from previous verse 'He found you in need'
Added alif maqsurah (ى with small alif) at the end to show this is the final form
This is a common particle used to introduce a topic or condition. The shadda emphasizes the م.
Adding ا at the end makes this word mean 'as for' and helps connect it to what comes next

steps.m: and as for (introduces topic before لَا تَقْهَرْ)
steps.n: The فَ connects this to what follows, introducing a new topic that will be discussed. In this ayah, it introduces the topic of the orphan before the command not to oppress them.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the past tense verb
Added ي between second and third root letters to describe someone affected by the condition

steps.m: the one who is without parents
steps.n: Added ٱلْ to specify a particular orphan (the one being discussed)

steps.m: the orphan (object of فَلَا تَقْهَرْ - so do not oppress)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this is the object being acted upon by the verb تَقْهَرْ (oppress/subjugate)
This is a negative particle used with commands to tell someone not to do something. Here it's telling us not to oppress the orphan.

steps.m: so/then do not (command for تَقْهَرْ - oppress)
steps.n: The فَ shows this command comes as a result of what was mentioned before - because the person being addressed has been given blessings (mentioned in previous verses), they should therefore not oppress the orphan.
Added vowels to connect the root letters together and show this happened in the past
Added تَ at start to show 'you' are doing it, and changed vowels to show it happens in the present

steps.m: you dominate forcefully (as a negative command with لَا)
steps.n: Changed the ending to sukun (ْ) because it comes after لَا (do not) in the ayah, making it a command not to do something
This is a particle that introduces a new topic or condition. It's used to shift attention to something new, like saying 'Now, about...' in English

steps.m: and as for... (introduces the beggar mentioned in ٱلسَّآئِلَ)
steps.n: The و connects this with what came before. Here it introduces how to treat the beggar (ٱلسَّآئِلَ) mentioned right after this word.
This is the basic meaning of the root
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Changed to describe someone who does the asking
Added ال at the beginning to make it specific, and doubled the س with shadda because it comes after ال

steps.m: the one who asks (acted on by تَنْهَرْ)
steps.n: The word got a fatha mark at the end because it's being acted on by the verb 'do not repel'. The middle alif was written longer, but pronunciation stays the same.
This is a negative particle that tells us not to do the action mentioned after it (تَنْهَرْ - repel/drive away)

steps.m: so/then do not (negates تَنْهَرْ - repel/drive away)
steps.n: The فَ shows this command is a result of what was mentioned before about the beggar (ٱلسَّآئِلَ). It connects the two ideas: 'As for the beggar, so do not repel him.'
This is the basic meaning of the root
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the past tense verb

steps.m: you repel (in فَلَا تَنْهَرْ - so do not repel)
steps.n: Added تَ at beginning to show 'you' as subject, changed vowels to show it's a command with لا (don't)

steps.m: as for... (introduces the topic that will be discussed in فَحَدِّثْ)
steps.n: This particle introduces a new topic that will be discussed in the following command فَحَدِّثْ (then proclaim/speak). It's like saying 'Now, regarding...' before making a statement.

steps.m: And as for... (introduces the topic that will be discussed in فَحَدِّثْ)
steps.n: The و connects this phrase to what came before it, showing this is a continuation of the previous discussion
Root letters put together to make the noun form meaning 'blessing'
Changed the first vowel and added ة to make it refer to one specific instance of blessing

steps.m: by the blessing (of رَبِّكَ - your Lord)
steps.n: Added بِ at start to mean 'by/with' and ِ at end because it's connected to 'your Lord' coming after it
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
The doubled middle letter (shadda) makes this an intensive form, emphasizing the complete nature of the nurturing

The كَ means 'your' (when speaking to one male). The kasra (ِ) is added before the suffix for proper connection. This word is in the genitive case because it comes after نِعْمَة in a possession relationship, where نِعْمَة (blessing) possesses رَبّ (Lord).
Connected the root letters with fatha to create basic past tense verb
Added shadda and kasra to create emphatic command form
Added sukun to complete command form
Added فَ at beginning to show this command follows from previous statement
